text: Dispute Resolution in Rental Matters Common disputes: - Rent payment issues - Deposit refund - Maintenance responsibilities Resolution methods: - Mutual agreement - Mediation - Rent Controller or court Legal authorities: - Rent Control Court - Civil Court Documentation is important: - Lease agreement - Payment records

Illegal Eviction Practices Illegal eviction includes: - Forcefully removing tenant - Locking tenant out - Disconnecting utilities - Threats or harassment Legal eviction requires: - Valid reason - Proper notice - Approval from Rent Controller Tenants can take legal action against illegal eviction.

Common Legal Terms in Rental Law Tenant: Person who rents property. Landlord: Owner of property. Lease: Agreement between tenant and landlord. Security Deposit: Money paid as guarantee. Eviction: Legal removal of tenant. Notice Period: Time before ending agreement. Fair Rent: Rent fixed by authority. Subletting: Tenant renting property to another person.

Maintenance Responsibilities Tenant Responsibilities: - Keep property clean - Minor repairs (bulbs, small fixes) - Avoid property damage Landlord Responsibilities: - Major repairs (structure, plumbing) - Maintain essential services - Ensure property is livable Disputes may arise if: - Responsibilities are not clearly defined in agreement

Notice Period Rules in Rental Agreements - Notice period is usually 1 to 3 months. - Both tenant and landlord must follow agreed notice period. Tenant must: - Inform landlord before vacating - Clear dues before leaving Landlord must: - Give notice before eviction (except legal eviction cases) Failure to follow notice: - May result in loss of deposit or penalties

Property Damage Rules Tenant must: - Avoid intentional damage - Inform landlord of major issues Landlord can: - Deduct repair cost from deposit Normal wear and tear: - Cannot be charged to tenant Examples: - Broken fixtures → chargeable - Paint fading → not chargeable

Rent Increase Rules - Rent increase must follow agreement terms. - Sudden or arbitrary increase is not allowed. Factors affecting increase: - Market conditions - Property value - Agreement clauses Tenant rights: - Can dispute unfair increases - Can approach Rent Controller Written agreements help avoid disputes.

Lease Agreement The tenant agrees to pay a monthly rent of ₹15,000 on or before the 5th of each month. A refundable security deposit of ₹30,000 shall be paid at the time of signing. The tenant must provide a 2-month notice before vacating the premises. The landlord may deduct from the deposit for damages beyond normal wear and tear.

Security Deposit Rules in Telangana - Security deposit is typically 2 to 3 months of rent. - Deposit must be refunded after tenant vacates. - Deductions allowed only for: - Property damage beyond normal wear and tear - Unpaid rent or utility bills Landlord must: - Provide explanation for deductions - Return remaining amount within reasonable time Unfair practices: - Holding deposit without reason - Excessive deductions

Subletting Rules - Tenant cannot sublet property without landlord permission. - Unauthorized subletting is a valid eviction ground. If allowed: - Must be clearly mentioned in agreement Risks: - Legal complications - Eviction Both parties must agree before subletting.

The Telangana Buildings (Lease, Rent and Eviction) Control Act, 1960 forms the primary legal framework governing rental housing in the state of Telangana. The purpose of this law is to regulate the relationship between landlords and tenants, ensure fair rental practices, and prevent exploitation on either side. Over time, rental practices have evolved, but the core principles of fairness, transparency, and legal protection remain central to tenancy laws. In Telangana, a tenancy is typically established through a rental agreement between the landlord and tenant.

gana, a tenancy is typically established through a rental agreement between the landlord and tenant. This agreement may be written or oral, although written agreements are strongly preferred as they provide clarity and legal enforceability. A typical rental agreement includes details such as the monthly rent, duration of tenancy, security deposit, maintenance responsibilities, and notice period required for termination. Tenants are granted protection from arbitrary eviction under the law. A landlord cannot simply remove a tenant without valid legal grounds.

Common grounds for eviction include non-payment of rent, misuse of the premises, violation of agreement terms, or the landlord’s genuine need for the property. Even when such grounds exist, eviction must follow due legal process, often involving notice and, in some cases, court proceedings. The concept of fair rent is an important aspect of tenancy regulation. If a tenant believes that the rent being charged is excessive, they may approach the appropriate authority to determine fair rent.

This includes maintaining structural integrity, ensuring proper electrical wiring, plumbing systems, and sanitation facilities. If major repairs are required, the responsibility generally lies with the landlord. Minor repairs and day-to-day maintenance are typically the responsibility of the tenant.

Utility Rules for Tenants - Tenants must pay electricity, water, and gas bills. - Bills should be paid on time. Landlord cannot: - Disconnect utilities without legal reason - Use utilities to force eviction Disputes: - Should be resolved through agreement or legal authority
